title: How to Dress for Golf in 2026: Performance Meets Style
excerpt: Golf fashion has evolved dramatically. Here's how to look great on the course without sacrificing performance.
content:
Golf apparel has come a long way from stiff collared shirts and plaid trousers. Today's golfer demands clothing that performs under pressure and looks sharp doing it. The best modern golf brands — and we'd put ourselves in that category — design every piece to work as hard as you do, whether you're on the practice green or in the clubhouse after your round.

The key shift in golf fashion over the last five years has been the move toward athletic cuts and lifestyle crossover. A great golf polo today should look just as natural at a weekend brunch as it does on the 18th fairway. That means cleaner lines, more considered colourways, and fabrics that drape like a woven rather than clinging like traditional technical wear.

For men, the winning formula in 2026 is a fitted polo, a tailored short or pant, and a clean cap. Avoid loud prints unless you're committed to the bit — subtle tonal branding and clean graphics read better in person and in photos. For women, the trend is toward relaxed-fit tops, longer skorts, and layering pieces that transition from the course to the patio without a wardrobe change.

Colour-wise, we're seeing earth tones dominate — sage, sand, camel, cream, and warm grey. These tones work with natural environments and photograph beautifully. Bold colours still have a place, but they're being used as accents rather than head-to-toe statements.

Perhaps most importantly, fit matters more than anything. A technically superior fabric in an ill-fitting cut will always lose to a simpler fabric that fits well. When you're trying on golf clothes, simulate your swing. Can you rotate freely? Does the shirt stay tucked? Does the waistband dig in when you bend? The best golf outfit is the one you're not thinking about when you're playing.

title: Building a Golf Capsule Wardrobe: 8 Pieces, All Season
excerpt: You don't need a closet full of golf clothes. Here are the 8 essential pieces that cover you from spring to fall.
content:
The idea of a capsule wardrobe — a small collection of versatile, high-quality pieces that work together — translates perfectly to golf. Most golfers wear the same few items on rotation anyway. The goal is to make those pieces really good.

Here's the Sakaki Golf capsule: two to three polos in neutral tones (white, sage, charcoal), one pair of tailored golf pants, two pairs of shorts in complementary colours, one mid-layer (a quarter-zip or light jacket), one cap, and a belt. That's it. Eight or nine pieces that mix, match, and cover you from early April through late October in most climates.

The key to a capsule that works is buying in a coordinated colour palette. If your polos are white, sage, and charcoal, your shorts should be sand, navy, and charcoal. Everything works with everything else. You never open your golf bag wondering if your shirt matches your pants.

Investing in quality over quantity also means your clothes last longer and hold their shape better. A $95 polo that you wear 40 times over two seasons is a much better value than a $40 polo you replace every year.

Finally, take care of your clothes. Golf apparel — especially technical fabrics — performs best when laundered properly. Cold wash, hang dry. Skip the fabric softener on moisture-wicking pieces — it coats the fibres and reduces breathability. A properly cared-for golf wardrobe should serve you well for several seasons.

title: Why Fit Matters More Than Fabric in Golf Apparel
excerpt: The most technically advanced fabric means nothing if the cut restricts your swing. Here's what to look for.
content:
Every golf apparel brand talks about fabric technology. Moisture-wicking. UPF protection. Four-way stretch. These things matter — but they matter a lot less than fit. A well-fitted polo in a mid-range fabric will always outperform a technically superior shirt that binds at the shoulders or pulls across the back.

The golf swing is a complex, full-rotation movement. Your clothing needs to accommodate that movement without restriction. When you're evaluating fit in a golf shirt, run through these checks: raise both arms overhead — is there any pulling at the hem or shoulder? Simulate your backswing rotation — does the shirt stay tucked, and is there any restriction across the upper back? Reach forward as if addressing the ball — does the collar gap or the shirt ride up?

For bottoms, the key checkpoints are: can you complete a full hip rotation without the waistband digging in? Is the thigh cut generous enough for a full weight shift? Does the waistband sit comfortably through a four-hour round?

Fit preference varies by player. Shorter hitters who prioritize mobility often prefer a relaxed fit. Players who care more about aesthetics on the course tend to prefer a trimmer silhouette. Neither is wrong — the best golf clothes are the ones you genuinely enjoy wearing, because that confidence translates to how you carry yourself on the course.

At Sakaki, we cut our polos with a relaxed-athletic fit: trim enough to look intentional, with enough room through the chest and shoulders to swing freely. Our shorts and bottoms all have a functional four-way stretch and are tested through a full range of motion before finalizing any pattern.
